item: omnia-export-guards: iter_u8_new / iter__new<T> accept base=0 with len>0, returning a poisoned-but-valid handle
reachability_fact: STDLIB/iii/omnia/iter.iii:25-40 (iter_u8_new) and :109-122 (iter__new<T>): no guard on `base == 0u64`. When called as `iter_u8_new(0u64, 5u64)`, the function scans ITER_LIVE, finds a free slot, stores ITER_BASE[slot]=0, ITER_LEN[slot]=5, ITER_LIVE[slot]=1, and returns `(slot as u64) + 1u64` (e.g. 1u64) — a non-zero value that callers treat as success. Only iter_u8_next (line 59: `if base == 0u64 { return ITER_END }`) and iter__next<T> (line 131: `if base == 0u64 { return 0u64 }`) guard base at read time. There is no construction-time gate. Corpus 11, 20, and 397 all call iter_u8_new / iter__new<T> with non-null base so they never exercise the null-base path; the gap is undetected by existing KATs. crystal_deps.iii exports guard at lines 103-105, 127-129, 192-194, 282-284. crystal_edges.iii exports guard via crystal_slot_of_pub + CE_BASE checks at lines 46-48, 64-69, 88-91, 94-97, 100-105, 121-123. hw_offload_register guards at line 114 (`if l_pp == 0u64 { return HW_OFFLOAD_E_NULL }`). Those three modules are already correct; only iter.iii needs the fix.
maximal_design: Add a construction-time null-base guard to BOTH iter_u8_new and iter__new<T>, consistent with the entry-guard idiom that every other omnia @export uses. The guard reads: `if base == 0u64 && len != 0u64 { return ITER_INVALID }`. The len==0 carveout is correct: an empty iterator over a null base never derefs memory — refusing it would be wrong. The guard must appear AFTER the slot scan (so no slot is claimed) and BEFORE writing into the slot tables. This makes the construction API honest: a non-ITER_INVALID return now guarantees that either len==0 (trivially exhausted) or base is non-null and next() will deref safely. The complementary read-time guards in iter_u8_next and iter__next<T> remain as defence-in-depth for stale handles that survive arena reset. No other changes to the slot tables, iter_slot_of, or any other @export are required — the fix is localized entirely to the two construction paths. The KAT proves the negative: iter_u8_new(0, 5) must return ITER_INVALID (0); iter_u8_new(0, 0) must still succeed (empty iter). No ABI change: return type u64, ITER_INVALID==0 is already the documented failure sentinel. Callers (corpus 11, 20, 397) all pass malloc'd non-null bases so they are unaffected.

code_changes.anchor: fn iter_u8_new(base: u64, len: u64) -> u64 @export {
fn iter__new<T>(base: u64, len: u64) -> u64 @specialize(u16, u32, u64, i8, i16, i32, i64) @export {

kat: Corpus 1023 (STDLIB/corpus/1023_iter_null_base_guard.iii). Three assertions prove the fix: (1) NEGATIVE: `iter_u8_new(0u64, 5u64)` must return 0u64 (ITER_INVALID) — fails on the unfixed code (returns 1u64), passes after fix; (2) BOUNDARY: `iter_u8_new(0u64, 0u64)` must return non-zero and signal ITER_END on next() — a zero-length empty iter over a null base is always safe; (3) POSITIVE: `iter_u8_new(malloc'd ptr, 3u64)` yields the three stored bytes in order — proves the normal path is unchanged. The negative case (assertion 1, return code 1) is what proves the fix fires. Existing corpus 11 (iter_u8_count), 20 (iter_u8_skip), and 397 (iter_specialize) all pass non-null bases so they continue to be GREEN and verify no regression.
